summary: IAEA reported a massive attack on the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, highlighting risks to the power grid. Russian forces continued extensive UAV strikes across multiple fronts, including hits on infrastructure in Kyiv, Kherson, and Crimea. The United States advanced a $8 billion aid package for Ukraine while Russian intelligence indicated a capacity to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles monthly.
key_points: IAEA observed heavy smoke and sounds of military activity at Zaporizhzhia TPP, noting personnel in shelters and concerns over the plant's transmission line (Source: @UkraineNow/81666).
Multiple Russian‑controlled UAVs were reported over Kharkiv, Zaporizhzhia, Sumy, and other regions, targeting cities, military assets, and infrastructure (Source: @kpszsu/64058).
Russia claims the ability to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month and plans to deliver up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).
U.S. House of Representatives backed a $8 billion military credit package and additional sanctions against Russia, with a final vote expected on 7 August (Source: @UkraineNow/81657).
Ukrainian UAVs struck a Russian patrol boat class "Svitlyak" in Crimea and hit a Pantsir‑S1 air defence system in Kherson region (Source: @UkraineNow/81667).
Russian attacks in Zaporizhzhia caused ten civilian injuries, damaging a residential high‑rise and a sleeping district (Source: @UkraineNow/81681).
Finland warned it was prepared to shoot down Ukrainian drones that might enter its airspace after a strike on St. Petersburg (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30666).

sections.executive_summary: IAEA reported a massive attack on the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, raising concerns over the plant's transmission line. Russian UAV operations intensified, striking infrastructure across Kyiv, Kharkiv, Zaporizhzhia, Sumy, and Crimea. The United States moved forward with a multi‑billion‑dollar aid package while Russia announced a capacity to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles monthly.

points: The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) observed light smoke and heard military sounds at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, confirming plant personnel were sheltering due to the attack and that the plant's single transmission line remains connected (Source: @UkraineNow/81666).

Ukrainian forces reported a UAV strike on a Russian patrol boat of the "Svitlyak" class in Crimea, also damaging a Pantsir‑S1 air‑defence system in the Kherson region and a radar navigation system RSBN‑4N (Source: @UkraineNow/81667).
A Russian attack on Zaporizhzhia resulted in ten civilian injuries, with damage to a residential high‑rise and a sleeping district (Source: @UkraineNow/81681).

Ukrainian officials announced that the United States House of Representatives supported a $8 billion military credit package and additional sanctions, with a final vote slated for 7 August (Source: @UkraineNow/81657).
Finland's defence minister stated the country was ready to intercept Ukrainian drones over its airspace following a Ukrainian strike on St. Petersburg (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30666).

Russian intelligence disclosed the ability to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month and a production rate of 55–60 missiles monthly, with plans to deliver up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).

Russian forces carried out coordinated UAV attacks on multiple Ukrainian regions, including Kyiv (Boryspil industrial site), Kherson (ambulance vehicle), and Sumy (village of Yampil) causing civilian casualties (Source: @UkraineNow/81677).

Russia paid 204.3 billion rubles in subsidies to its oil refineries in May, a significant increase from previous months, to offset fuel price differentials caused by the war (Source: @UkraineNow/81662).

Russian officials claimed a closed‑loop dialogue with the United States over Ukrainian settlement, citing interest from Kushner and Whitfield, though no concrete outcomes were reported (Source: @tass_agency/379206).
Ukrainian UAVs were reported over Kharkiv region, flying past Krasnopavlivka heading west, and over Sumy from the north (Source: @kpszsu/64058).
In the Kharkiv direction, a guided strike UAV approached from the southwest toward the city (Source: @kpszsu/64027).
Ukrainian forces noted UAV activity over the Melitopol urban district, with a massive attack on the district and a school hit, injuring six civilians (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30663).
Russian UAV attacks targeted the Melitopol meat‑processing plant, causing one injury (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30663).
Ukrainian UAVs were observed over Chernihiv region near Slavutych and over Sumy direction from the north (Source: @eRadarrua/95441; @kpszsu/64058).
In the north‑west of Dnipropetrovsk region, UAVs flew past the settlement of Pyatykhatky (Source: @kpszsu/64028).
Ukrainian UAVs were reported near Konotop heading toward Baturyn in Sumy region (Source: @kpszsu/64021).
Ukrainian UAVs over Chernihiv region passed near Slavutych, indicating continued aerial pressure on northern oblasts (Source: @eRadarrua/95441).
Ukrainian UAVs attacked a Russian patrol boat in Crimea and a Pantsir‑S1 system in Kherson, showing capability to strike naval and air‑defence assets in the south (Source: @UkraineNow/81667).
Russian UAVs targeted an industrial facility in the Boryspil area of Kyiv region, causing a worker injury and fire (Source: @UkraineNow/81655).
Ukrainian forces reported a UAV strike on a Russian ambulance in Kherson, wounding three medical workers (Source: @UkraineNow/81677).
Ukrainian UAVs were observed over the Black Sea heading toward the Odesa region (Karolino‑Buhaz) (Source: @kpszsu/64060).
More than 20 Ukrainian drones were shot down over Sevastopol during a large‑scale UAV attack on Crimea (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30663).
Ukrainian UAVs conducted attacks on Russian air‑defence radars, including a Pantsir‑S1 system in Kherson and a radar navigation system RSBN‑4N in Crimea (Source: @UkraineNow/81667).
Russian forces launched a coordinated UAV barrage across multiple fronts, including Kyiv, Kharkiv, Sumy, and the Black Sea, indicating a sustained aerial campaign (Source: @kpszsu/64058).
Ukrainian air‑defence systems engaged and downed UAVs over Sevastopol and other southern locations, demonstrating active air‑defence response (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30663).

key_developments: IAEA reported heavy smoke and military sounds at Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, with staff in shelters and concerns over the plant's transmission line (Source: @UkraineNow/81666).

Russia announced the capability to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month and a production rate of 55–60 missiles, planning to deliver up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).
Ukrainian UAVs struck a Russian patrol boat class "Svitlyak" in Crimea and damaged a Pantsir‑S1 air‑defence system in Kherson (Source: @UkraineNow/81667).
The U.S. House of Representatives backed an $8 billion military credit package and additional sanctions against Russia, with a final vote set for 7 August (Source: @UkraineNow/81657).
Multiple Russian UAV attacks hit civilian infrastructure across Zaporizhzhia, Kherson, and Sumy, causing injuries and property damage (Source: @UkraineNow/81677).
Finland prepared to intercept Ukrainian drones over its airspace after a Ukrainian strike on St. Petersburg (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30666).
More than 20 Ukrainian drones were shot down over Sevastopol during a large‑scale UAV assault on Crimea (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30663).
sections.threat_assessment: Russian missile production capacity and declared launch rates suggest a sustained strategic strike capability, while the proliferation of UAV attacks across multiple fronts indicates an escalating low‑altitude threat to Ukrainian command, control and civilian infrastructure. Ukrainian counter‑UAV successes in Crimea demonstrate growing air‑defence proficiency but also highlight the intensity of Russian aerial operations. The involvement of external actors, such as Finland's readiness to engage Ukrainian drones, adds a regional dimension to the air conflict.
sections.regional_response: Western allies, notably the United States, are advancing substantial financial and military aid, reflecting continued support for Ukrainian defence capabilities. Russian subsidies to its oil refineries aim to mitigate economic strain from sanctions and sustain war‑time production. Localised Ukrainian resilience, such as sheltering plant personnel and rapid fire‑suppression at industrial sites, underscores adaptive civil‑military coordination.
sections.intelligence_notes: Monitoring of IAEA reports on Zaporizhzhia remains critical for assessing nuclear safety risks. UAV activity logs across the north, east, and south fronts provide actionable data for targeting Russian command nodes and air‑defence assets. The announced Russian ballistic missile launch capacity warrants heightened alert for potential long‑range strikes, especially in conjunction with Kinzhal deployments.
